experiments.title: How removing the organization’s name from an email sender impacts engagement during a high urgency campaign

rows.name: From: Jody at Bible League Canada

experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesized that removing” at Bible League Canada” from the email sender name would improve the open rate of their emails. This would make the email look and feel more like a one-to-one communication in the inbox instead of an organization’s “marketing” email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2024 - 04/29/2024
Bible League Canada (BLC) is a ministry organization that uses members of its staff as email senders. The sender name for these staff emails always includes the person’s name and” at Bible League Canada” after it. The content of BLC’s emails feels very authentic and has helped foster strong relationships with their email list subscribers. Still, we wanted to collect data to understand whether including their organization’s name in their email sender name was hurting their email open rates.
We decided to test this concept during one of BLC’s high-urgency campaigns. We tested it with a direct appeal email sent from Hannah, one of Bible League Canada’s main email senders. The results of that test can be found here.
We wanted to retest this hypothesis using another sender during the campaign to see if the positive results we discovered would be limited to the sender we chose.
We ran this as an A/B email test, with 50% of BLC’s housefile list receiving the control email and the other 50% receiving the treatment email. The email’s subject line and content were the same for both variants; the only variable changed was the email sender’s name. These were the variants in this test:
Control Email – From name was “Jody at Bible League Canada”
Treatment Email – From name was “Jody”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the organization's name from the email sender's name can significantly increase open rates. The treatment email, with the sender name "Jody," saw a 40.8% increase in opens compared to the control email, "Jody at Bible League Canada." This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating a strong impact.
We could not validate an increase in clicks from the treatment version. With this second test result, Bible League Canada's audience appears to respond well when appeals are sent without the "at Bible League Canada" postscript. This test should be revisited in the future to see if it is a "fad" or something that remains constant for their email file. But for now, using personal, authentic sender names should be the template for email cultivation and activation.

experiments.title: What is the impact of personalizing Agenda articles based on website history on Clicks and Opens?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing Immigration content in The Agenda for subscribers with Heritage website history related to crime, immigration, and national security will achieve a significant increase in the click/open rate because we personalizing content based of their previous engagement with heritage.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/22/2025
The membership team conducted a series of tests to create a personalized Agenda Email Newsletter based on the subscribers website history using Marketo data.
Two personalization groups were created based on keywords from subscribers Heritage website history. Group A – focused on subscribers with website history associated with the economy and Group B – focused on subscribers with website history associated with National Security, Crime, and Immigration.
Testing segments breakdown:
Group A – Control (25%)- received the “national security version” with articles related to the national security being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter
Group A (75%)- Treatment – received the “economy version” with articles related to the economy being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter
Group B (25%)- Control – received the “economy version” with articles related to the economy being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter,
Group B (75%)- Treatment – received the “national security version” with articles related to the national security being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter
In every test, the “National Security” personalization group outperformed the “Economy” group and so the metrics reported in this test only include results from Group B.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that prioritizing Immigration content in The Agenda for subscribers with Heritage website history related to crime, immigration, and national security significantly increases the click/open rate. The treatment group, which received the "national security version" with articles related to national security being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter, saw a 46.5% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group. This result was achieved with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating a strong significance in the impact of the personalized content.

In future experimentation, we should continue to personalize content based on subscribers' previous engagement with the Heritage website. By analyzing subscribers' website history and tailoring the content to their specific interests, we can enhance engagement and drive higher click-through rates. It is essential to continue testing different personalization strategies to understand what resonates best with our audience and refine our approach based on the results. This experiment demonstrates the effectiveness of personalized content in driving engagement and highlights the importance of catering to subscribers' individual interests to optimize performance.

experiments.title: How testing whether an urgency-driven subject line or a specific biblical reference leads to higher opens and engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a specific and intriguing biblical reference (“Discover the richness of Exodus”) instead of a more generic faith-based phrase (“Don’t miss this opportunity to deepen your faith”) for people viewing a content offer email in their inbox will achieve an increase in email opens because specificity and curiosity drive engagement, making the subject line feel more relevant and compelling to the reader.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2025 - 02/08/2025
Luther Seminary creates ebooks and digital resources to help their audience deepen their faith and biblical knowledge. Their latest ebook explores the book of Exodus, and they aimed to maximize engagement by optimizing the subject line of their promotional email to their housefile audience.
To determine what type of subject line would drive more opens and clicks, we conducted a series of A/B tests. In a prior test, we compared exclusivity-based language to a curiosity-driven subject line, finding that a curiosity-driving subject line increased opens by 9.4% with 100% statistical confidence.
For this next test, we wanted to know whether an urgency-based subject line (“Don’t miss this opportunity to deepen your faith”) or a subject line that was more specific and intriguing (“Discover the richness of Exodus”) would drive higher engagement. We hypothesized that the more specific and curiosity-driven subject line would increase opens by making the content feel more relevant and compelling to the reader.
This was a 50/50 A/B test, with half of the audience receiving the Control subject line and the other half receiving the Treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test produced unexpected but valuable insights about the relationship between subject line messaging and user engagement.

While the Control subject line ("Don't miss this opportunity to deepen your faith") generated more opens, the Treatment subject line ("Discover the richness of Exodus") resulted in fewer opens (a 3.8% decrease) but led to a 24.5% increase in email clicks, with a 98.4% statistical level of confidence.

This suggests that while urgency-driven language in the Control created a stronger initial impulse to open the email, the specificity of the Treatment subject line attracted a more engaged audience—those who opened were more likely to click through to the content.

Psychologically, this aligns with two key principles:
FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) and urgency – The Control subject line may have driven more opens by making the email feel time-sensitive and broadly relevant.
 	Relevance and commitment – The Treatment subject line set clearer expectations about the content, likely attracting recipients who were already interested in the topic, leading to higher engagement post-open.

experiments.title: How comparing a curiosity-driven subject line to an exclusivity-based subject line to determine impact on opens and engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a vague, curiosity-driven subject line (“Here’s your first look”) instead of an exclusivity-based subject line (“Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide”) for people viewing a content offer email in their inbox will achieve an increase in email opens because the curiosity gap encourages recipients to open the email to learn more.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 01/17/2025
Luther Seminary develops useful ebooks and other digital resources that help their audience deepen their faith and knowledge of the Bible. They recently created a new ebook about the book of Exodus and wanted to send it to their housefile email audience.
We wanted to know what kind of subject line would drive more opens and clicks for this content offer email, so we decided to run an A/B test.
The Control subject line used exclusivity messaging: “Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide.” The Treatment subject line was shorter and designed to spark curiosity: “Here’s your first look.”
We hypothesized that the curiosity-driven subject line would generate more opens by enticing recipients to learn more.
This was a 50/50 A/B test, with half of the audience receiving the Control subject line and the other half receiving the Treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment confirmed that a curiosity-driven subject line can significantly increase email opens. The Treatment subject line ("Here's your first look") outperformed the Control ("Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide") by increasing opens 9.4%, with a 100% statistical level of confidence.

The success of the curiosity-based subject line aligns with the Curiosity Gap Theory, which suggests that people feel compelled to seek out missing information when presented with an incomplete or intriguing statement. Unlike the Control, which relied on exclusivity messaging, the Treatment sparked interest without revealing too much—prompting recipients to open the email to learn more.

However, while opens increased, click rates remained the same (~2.2% for both versions). This suggests that while curiosity successfully drove more people to open the email, it did not necessarily increase engagement with the content inside. A possible explanation is that the Treatment subject line created intrigue but did not set clear expectations about the offer, leading some recipients to open the email without a strong intention to engage further.

To build on this learning, future tests should explore:
Refining curiosity-driven subject lines – Testing variations that maintain intrigue while hinting more explicitly at the content's value.
 	Combining curiosity with faith-based language – Testing curiosity-driven phrasing with biblical references to see if it enhances both opens and clicks. Update: We ran a follow-up experiment to test curiosity-based language against a subject line featuring a specific biblical reference to see which approach is more effective at engaging faith-driven audiences.
 	Aligning email content with subject line expectations – Ensuring that the email itself delivers on the curiosity generated in the inbox, potentially increasing clicks.

experiments.title: How a personalized sender impacts open and click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personalized sender for Franciscan University’s emails will achieve an increase in opens and click-thru rate because it humanizes the entire interaction—addressing the reader by name and writing from the perspective of a real person. because it humanizes the entire interaction—addressing the reader by name and writing from the perspective of a real person.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2025
Historically, the majority of Franciscan University’s email have come from the organization itself. They do not address the reader by name with a salutation, are written in a third-person tone, and do not come from a specific sender at the University. We hypothesized that personalizing this experience for the reader: addressing them by name in the opening, sending from a real person, and humanizing the email copy so it read like an email rather than a newsletter would increase opens and click-thru rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that personalizing the sender for Franciscan University's emails had a significant impact on the open rates, resulting in a 33.5% increase in opens for all traffic with a 100.0% level of statistical confidence. This outcome suggests that humanizing the email interaction by addressing the reader by name and sending from a real person resonated positively with the recipients and encouraged them to engage more with the emails.

Additionally, our second hypothesis is that this would increase click-thru rate as well as opens. While just below statistical validity, Franciscan also saw a 12% increase in click-thru rate with a 93.9% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is advisable to continue personalizing the sender for Franciscan University's emails to maintain or even further improve the open and click-thru rates. However, it may be worthwhile to determine whether this approach results in the same effect on Faith and Reason emails—which users are more accustomed to receiving from the organization itself. Regularly testing and analyzing different tactics will provide valuable insights for optimizing email communications and achieving the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: Does updating the cover for a content offer to be clearer affect email acquisition?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that updating the ebook cover on the landing page to a more designed and easy-to-read version would lead to higher email acquisition rates because the improved visual clarity and appeal would better engage users and encourage them to complete the form.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2025
This experiment was conducted for the SETI Institute, a nonprofit organization dedicated to scientific research and education in the search for life beyond Earth. The focus of this test was on the landing page, where the ebook cover image was updated from a photo (control) to a more designed and easy-to-read version (test). The goal was to evaluate whether the updated cover would improve email acquisition by enhancing the user experience on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Main Takeaway
The test version of the landing page, featuring the updated ebook cover, acquired 19% more emails than the control, with a confidence level of 99.2%. This result demonstrates that the more visually appealing cover design positively impacted user engagement and lead generation.
Observations:

 	The redesigned cover likely improved the user’s first impression of the landing page, making the offer more compelling and increasing trust in the content.
 	The consistent visual alignment between the ad and the landing page may have reinforced the user’s decision to engage with the form.

Next Steps

 	Adopt the New Cover Design: Implement the updated cover design on all landing pages to improve email acquisition rates across campaigns.
 	A/B Test Additional Landing Page Elements: Evaluate other design and content changes, such as CTA language, form placement, or page layout, to identify further optimization opportunities.
 	Monitor Long-Term Impact: Assess the quality of leads acquired through the updated landing page to ensure sustained performance improvements downstream.

experiments.title: How an aligned, value-driven image of an ebook on a landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using imagery that shows the content of an ebook for users visiting an ebook landing page will achieve an increase in email sign-ups because the imagery will showcase the value and ebook content more effectively.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/31/2024
During the Christmas season, Mission Aviation Fellowship (MAF) was running a devotional ebook acquisition offer. When running an offer like this, the landing page will typically feature a 3D mockup image of the ebook cover to help users on the page understand what they are receiving.
For this devotional offer, the ads on Facebook used a “scattered pages” design to showcase some of the unique offerings inside the ebook (devotions, Bible verses, crossword puzzles, etc.). We wondered if using this imagery on the devotional ebook landing page would help to enhance the value proposition and increase conversions.
We ran this as an A/B test for 29 days, with 50% of landing page traffic seeing the image of the 3D ebook, and the other 50% of traffic seeing the “scattered pages” design.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that utilizing imagery that shows the content of an ebook on a landing page can significantly increase email sign-ups. In this case, the treatment version, which featured an image showcasing some of the unique offerings inside the ebook, led to a 35.5% increase in emails acquired compared to the control version that only showed the 3D ebook cover. This lift was at a 99.2% LOC, which means that the result is considered statistically valid.

In future experiments, it is important to continue testing imagery on the landing page that effectively showcases the value and content of the content offer. This can help capture the attention of users and communicate the benefits of the ebook more clearly, ultimately leading to higher conversion rates.

Further testing should also be done to determine the impact of aligning the imagery on the landing page with any imagery that users are seeing at the front of the funnel through ads, emails, MMS, etc.

experiments.title: How a less emotive subject line impacts the open rate of a sale email

rows.name: The true meaning of NIO Summit

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more direct subject line for email subscribers will achieve a higher open rate  because it’s clear what information they will find in the email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2024 - 08/02/2024
Prior to our NIO Summit 2024 flash sale, we sent out an email to give our subscribers a heads-up about the upcoming sale in case they needed to get budget approval.
In an attempt to get higher visibility on the sale, we tested the subject line to see if a more direct subject line would increase email opens.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a more direct subject line for this type of NIO Summit email can significantly increase open rates, as demonstrated by the 21.1% increase in opens with a 100.0% level of statistical confidence.
This indicates that subscribers are more likely to open an email when they have a clear understanding of the information they will find inside.
Furthermore, the treatment email also resulted in a 678% increase in Clicks with a 100% level of confidence and a 386% increase in Clicks to Opens with a 95% level of confidence.
This shows that not only did the more direct subject line improve open rates, but it also drove more engagement and actions downstream from subscribers.
The treatment subject line also creates a more intense sense of urgency while also not giving everything away. It creates a sense of mystery — "What's the announcement?"
So, moving on from this experiment, we will continue testing ways to create more direct and urgent subject lines that clearly articulate the message in the email without giving it all away.

experiments.title: How a slide out for a disaster appeal impacts donor conversion for website visitors compared with a sticky bar appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky donate button on the website with a slide-out disaster appeal CTA will increase donor conversion compared with a sticky bar appeal by reducing donor friction and increasing the urgency of making a gift during a natural disaster
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2023 - 09/23/2023
CBN’s Operation Blessing provides critical disaster relief supplies to communities devastated by natural disasters. Typically, when a disaster strikes, they place an urgent donation appeal sticky bar message on their website to promote giving. They team wanted to see if this was the most effective tactic for increasing donor conversion during disasters.
We hypothesized that the sticky bar could easily be missed due to “banner blindness” on the site, especially with how busy their pages were with many CTAs, images, etc. We decided to test a sticky donate button that would be on the right side of the screen and slide out a disaster relief appeal message with “yes” “no” buttons. We believed this would reduce friction and increase the urgency of the disaster appeal, leading to higher donor conversion.
The control message read, “Give today to help provide disaster relief for those affected by Hurricane Idalia.”
The treatment message read, “Hi there, can you take 30-seconds to provide clean water, hot food, and critical supplies to those impacted by Hurricane Idalia? 100% of your gift goes to meeting their needs.” It would slide out automatically after the user was on the page for 7 seconds. We ran the experiment for 5 days with traffic split evenly between both experiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a sticky donate button with a slide-out disaster appeal message significantly increases donor conversion compared to a sticky bar appeal. The treatment message, which included a more personalized and urgent appeal, resulted in a 39.4% increase in donations with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

When digging into results by segment, every single segment showed significant donor preference for the sticky button vs control.

Here are results with level of confidence (LOC) by segment:
Mobile: 56% increased donor conversion at a 100% LOC (145 vs 93 donations)
 	Desktop: 33% increased donor conversion at a 99% LOC (162 vs 123 donations)
 	New visitors: 20% increased donor conversion at a 94% LOC (146 vs 122 donations)
 	Returning visitors: 60% increased donor conversion at a 100% LOC (172 vs 107 donations)

experiments.title: How does “opening a loop” in the subject line affect open rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an ‘open loop’ subject line will increase open rates by piquing people’s curiosity and creating a desire to ‘close the loop’ by finding out more about the update to which it alluded.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/20/2024
Since the primary job of a subject line is to win the open, we wanted to know if opening a loop of curiosity could improve open rates by giving less away. The control in this experiment directly stated what was inside the email, while the treatment merely mentioned an update.
The idea was that by piquing someone’s curiosity, or ‘opening a loop,’ the human desire to close that loop by finding out more about the update would prompt more people to open. Traffic was split roughly 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Using an 'open loop' subject line produced a statistically valid 18.3% increase in open rates. By teasing an update without giving away specific information, people are more likely to be intrigued and feel the need to open the email to discover what the update is. This tactic taps into the human desire to close open loops and can be a powerful tool for increasing engagement.
The CTR of the treatment was lower (18.8%), but the sample size was insufficient to validate that outcome, so we will reserve drawing conclusions in that regard. In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of curiosity-driven subject lines and test against other concepts. We will also continue monitoring the impact on click-through rates. We should also consider if the length of the subject line (the treatment being shorter) played a part in this result.
Finally, there is probably a more effective way to run this test while mitigating the risk of diminished click-through rates by teasing the subject matter of the email without giving the content away, I.e., "This will boost donations" etc.

experiments.title: How adding a callout to the monthly tab on the donation form impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly callout for for potential donors will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it draws attention to the monthly option and adds value proposition of the impact of a monthly gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/14/2024
In an effort to boost donor conversion metrics on MSLF’s Searle Petition, we decided to test including a callout on the monthly tab of the form. We have tested this concept with other MSLF offers and seen positive results.
The callout was only shown to half of the traffic and it read: “By giving a monthly gift, you are not just supporting our legal team’s defense 365 days a year, but you are safeguarding your Constitutional rights, your family’s Constitutional rights, and the rights of countless others.”
We believe that adding a callout for recurring giving for potential donors will achieve an increase in recurring giving because it draws the visitor’s attention to the monthly option as well as provides additional value proposition of the impact of a monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a monthly callout for potential donors actually decreased recurring donations by 79.3% compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 97.1% confidence level, indicating a strong likelihood that the decrease was not due to random chance.

After reviewing other donor conversion metrics, we saw some very interesting results. For one-time gifts, we actually saw a 70.7% lift in donations at an 87.5% LOC. While we did not anticipate that a monthly callout would result in an increase in one-time gifts, it does suggest that there is a psychological concept that we could tap into.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to further explore donor motivations and preferences through qualitative research or surveys. Understanding donors' perspectives and how they perceive different messages can provide valuable insights for designing effective fundraising strategies. Additionally, continuing to test different variations and closely monitoring the results will help in optimizing donation forms for increased conversions in the future.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of a subject line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the clarity of the subject line for email respondents will achieve an increase in open rate because the appeal extends to a larger audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/14/2023
The Navigators were sending out a cultivation email as the initial email for their Sustainers campaign. The control email used the subject line “What group is on your heart today?” The team hypothesized that this subject line assumed that people understood what the organization meant by “group”, and that the reader did in fact have a group on their heart.
They proposed a treatment, “Who are you praying for today?” that would increase the clarity and appeal to a larger group of people.
experiments.keyLearnings:
From this experiment, we have learned that a more clear and relatable subject line in an email can lead to an increase in open rate and ultimately, in this case, a higher rate of donations.

The key learning from this experiment is that using language that appeals to a wider audience and is easily understood can be more effective in engaging recipients and motivating them to take action. The treatment subject line, "Who are you praying for?" was able to resonate with a larger group of people compared to the control subject line, "What group is on your heart today?". This likely contributed to the increased open rate and subsequent increase in donations.

In future experimentation, we should prioritize using clear and relatable language in our subject lines to capture the attention of recipients and encourage them to open the email. Additionally, we should continue to test different variations of subject lines to optimize our messaging and maximize engagement and conversion rates.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of crafting subject lines that are clear, relatable, and appealing to a wide audience. By continuing to test and refine our messaging strategies, we can optimize our email campaigns and drive greater impact for our nonprofit organization.

experiments.title: How offering a book as a premium with a donation vs at retail price impacts conversion rate and overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a new book as a premium gift in exchange for a donation rather than as a product for purchase for Time of Grace’s donor file will achieve an increase in transactions and revenue because users are more likely to be motivated by the generosity of the organization, and the value of receiving the gift for a donation.. because users are more likely to be motivated by the generosity of the organization, and the value of receiving the gift for a donation..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/16/2024
For Time of Grace’s monthly book promotion, we hypothesized that offering the book in exchange for a donation would result in increased transactions and revenue versus just offering it for purchase. Historically, Time of Grace has promoted their new books on their Shopify store at retail price. This test was accomplished by making a change to two areas of the funnel. The control vs treatment emails would have a change in the call-to-action copy. The control (for purchase) would lead them to Time of Grace’s Shopify page, whereas the treatment would lead them to a donation page that had the book as a premium on it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a new book as a premium gift in exchange for a donation, instead of selling it at retail price, significantly increased the number of donations received. The treatment group saw a 92.2% increase in donations compared to the control group, with a high statistical level of confidence.

Not only did this result in an increase in donor/purchaser conversion rate, it also resulted in a valid 199% increase in revenue with a 98.9% level of confidence.

Control: 27 Purchases | $996 in Revenue | $36.89 Average Gift

Treatment 52 Donations |$2,985 in Revenue | $57.40 Average Gift

This approach also did not limit the ministry's impact in actual copies "sold" with 53 units being purchased under the control and 55 units being requested as premiums with a donation.

This indicates that users are more motivated by the generosity of the organization and the perceived value of receiving a gift in exchange for their donation. Therefore, in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue offering premium gifts as incentives for donations.

This could be applied to other products or promotions as well, where offering a premium gift or exclusive content in exchange for a donation or purchase may motivate users to take action and increase overall transactions and revenue. The organization should consider implementing this strategy more frequently to leverage the increased motivation and generosity of their audience.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of a subject line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the clarity of the subject line for email respondents will achieve an increase in open rate because the appeal extends to a larger audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/14/2023
The Navigators were sending out a cultivation email as the initial email for their Sustainers campaign. The control email used the subject line “What group is on your heart today?” The team hypothesized that this subject line assumed that people understood what the organization meant by “group”, and that the reader did in fact have a group on their heart.
They proposed a treatment, “Who are you praying for today?” that would increase the clarity and appeal to a larger group of people.
experiments.keyLearnings:
From this experiment, we have learned that a more clear and relatable subject line in an email can lead to an increase in open rate and ultimately, in this case, a higher rate of donations.

The key learning from this experiment is that using language that appeals to a wider audience and is easily understood can be more effective in engaging recipients and motivating them to take action. The treatment subject line, "Who are you praying for?" was able to resonate with a larger group of people compared to the control subject line, "What group is on your heart today?". This likely contributed to the increased open rate and subsequent increase in donations.

In future experimentation, we should prioritize using clear and relatable language in our subject lines to capture the attention of recipients and encourage them to open the email. Additionally, we should continue to test different variations of subject lines to optimize our messaging and maximize engagement and conversion rates.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of crafting subject lines that are clear, relatable, and appealing to a wide audience. By continuing to test and refine our messaging strategies, we can optimize our email campaigns and drive greater impact for our nonprofit organization.

experiments.title: How your Facebook (Meta) Advertising performance goal impacts video campaign reach
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the “Ad recall lift” goal in Facebook (Meta) Advertising for our awareness campaign will achieve a more cost-effective reach  because the algorithm is showing it to whoever it believes will likely “remember” seeing the ad.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2023 - 03/15/2023
As a part of NextAfter’s “Fundraising Genius” branding ad campaign, our team wanted to experiment with the video awareness campaign goals to monitor what performance goal would achieve the greatest “reach” (which is defined as: how many people in the target audience received at least one ad impression) with the campaign.
So, our team decided to try the “Maximize number of impressions” goal (as the control) vs. a new awareness building campaign goal called “Maximize ad recall lift” (as the treatment).
Otherwise, the two campaigns were identical, targeting the same audience.
Our research questions really were:
Which campaign setting would generate the greatest reach, and to what amount are we trading off “frequency” of ad placements (eg how many times anyone in the audience sees an ad per day)? And,
Which of the two campaign types is more cost effective for the investment?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using the "Maximize ad recall lift" goal in Facebook advertising for awareness campaigns is more effective than using the "Maximize number of impressions" goal because it reached nearly 2x the number of people on just 12.8% of the spending of the control audience.
This was confirmed by a 91.3% increase in "Reach" (how many unique people in our target audience received at least one impression) with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.
To put that another way — the cost to reach 1,000 people in the control ad setting was $34.84, while the cost to reach 1,000 people with the treatment ad setting was just $2.68 (which is is a 92.3% decrease in cost to reach 1,000 people).
Having said all of that — it's worth noting that our frequency has plummeted, dropping from 12.4 in the control campaign to just 1.3 in the treatment.
Conclusion:
If you're looking to reach far more people in your target audience with at least one ad impression, prioritized to whomever the platform believes is most likely to consume the ad and remember it, you should use the "Awareness > Maximize ad recall lift" campaign setting.If you just want to overpower your competitors and ensure high ad frequency for a smaller number of your target audience, then you should use the "Awareness > Maximize the number of impressions" campaign setting.

experiments.title: How opening a loop of curiosity via the subject line affects open rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a less direct subject line will increase open rates by creating an open loop of curiosity that recipients will want to close by opening the emails to learn more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/14/2022
During a year-end campaign when we know potential donors will be receiving a high volume of emails soliciting donations, we wanted to know whether or not using less direct subject lines would improve open rates by opening a loop of curiosity that the recipient would want to close by opening the email.
Recipients of this campaign were segmented between previous donors and non-donors and roughly half of both segments were sent emails with subject lines that spoke directly to the contents of the email while the other half received emails that opened a loop of curiosity with less direct subject lines … but which (importantly) would still clearly relate to the subject matter of the email once the recipient opened. This test was run across three appeal emails in an effort to ensure the results were not isolated to a single send.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at the results of this experiment a number of interesting observations came to light:
The curiosity-building subject lines did indeed increase open rates by 2.6% (99.9% LoC)However, the curiosity-building subject lines also decreased click-through rates by -8.5% (97.4% LoC)
But because these sends were all segmented between previous donors and non-donors, drilling down into the data may help paint a more complete picture of our results, for instance:
Among non-donors:
Curiosity-building subject lines produced a 2.4% increase in open rates (100% LoC)But they also produced a 16.6% decrease in click-through rates  (100% LoC)
Among previous donors:
Curiosity-building subject lines produced a 4% increase in open rates (100% LoC)While also producing a 33.8% increase in click-through rates (99.9% LoC)
And so we see that not all recipients behave the same, further emphasizing the importance of tailoring communications to the audience that you are targeting, and doing away with the assumption that all audiences behave the same.
Non-donors may be less familiar with (or less bought into) the value American Cornerstone Institute provides—hence their status as non-donors—while previous donors, who better understand (or better align) with the value received from ACI were hooked by the curiosity-building subject line and continued their momentum through the appeal contained inside the email, ultimately clicking through to the donation page at a significantly higher rate.
Based on these results, we will want to send emails with direct subject lines to the non-donor segment while sending curiosity-building emails to the previous donor segments as the results from this experiment have clearly indicated each segment's preference.

experiments.title: How subject line urgency impacted email performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will have the biggest impact on subscriber engagement (opens and clicks)?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2016 - 01/13/2017
As the year end deadline approached for Illinois Policy Institute’s year end fundraising campaign, they wanted to find ways to communicate that urgency to the subscribers. For the second-to-last email send, sent out on December 30th, they decided to run a subject line test to determine which would have the biggest impact on subscriber engagement.
Both of the subject lines communicated urgency. The primary difference between the two was that one, time is running out, is more direct in its approach and is a more common approach taken by many organizations in their fundraising and marketing-style emails. The alternative, tick tock, was a bit less common and did not give away the purpose of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the more direct and “sale-y” subject line of time is running out decreased the open rate by 8%. Additionally, this lower open rate led to an incrementally lower click through rate with a 33% decrease (with 94.6% statistical validity). Our hypothesis is that with the time is running out subject line, the subscribers immediately felt marketed to since it is not a subject line most people would use in their day to day communications. As a result, they were less likely to engage.

This is a great example of the concept that people give to people. As fundraisers, we want to communicate urgency but we need to do it in such a way that it still feels genuine.

experiments.title: How subject line copy that “teases” affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line results in a higher open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/17/2015 - 11/20/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a free online course studying the Gospel of John. They were promoting it by renting email lists, and wanted to determine which subject lines encouraged more recipients to open the email. They developed two subject lines based on hypotheses about the prospect. The first was very direct: “Study the Gospel of John with DTS”. The second teased the offer, and expanded from the acronym: “A special offer from Dallas Theological Seminary”.
experiments.keyLearnings: This tells us that prospects are more likely to open an email when we tease the contents. While clarity has proven to trump persuasion over and over again, the subject line is a different element. It only has one job — to win the open. We can’t get people to click if they don’t open. So this suggests that teasing recipients of this email might increase open rate, which is the metric that affects every other following metric.

experiments.title: How the “lowest cost per click” conversion event impacted full video promotional ad views on Facebook Ads Manager
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the optimizing our video ad campaign for lowest cost per click for prospective and known audiences we were targeting in our ad campaign will achieve only increase clicks, but not increase full video completion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/10/2022
As a part of the NIO Summit 2022 promotional paid advertising campaign to sell event tickets, we wanted to experiment with what kind of video conversion event would produce the best balance between impressions, reach, clicks, and full promotional video views.
Our natural thought was to promote the video ads via “reach” campaign types, but our desire as direct response marketers was to test that natural inclination against a more “direct response” like conversion event of “lowest cost per click.”
How would the video ad start and finish rates play out while balanced against clicks and reach?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 100% level of confidence, we observed that full video views on the "lowest cost per click" campaign surprisingly produced a staggering increase of full video views in the amount of +851.9% over the "reach" optimized campaigns.
It's worth noting that we did reach 85,286 with the "reach" optimized campaign when compared to just 10,287 prospects in the "lowest cost per click" optimized campaign.
Alternatively, we did see 9.5x more clicks (+7805% increase in ad link clicks, with a 100% level of confidence) in the "lowest cost per click" optimized campaign, though.
So, to summarize — although we reached 8.2x more people with the "reach" campaign, we saw 9.5x more link clicks with the "lowest cpc" campaign (and 1.14x more "full video views" as noted in the data above) over the "reach" optimized campaign.
Ultimately, it comes down to what you value and your ultimate goal ... if you're looking to just get your video ads in front of the largest number of people — you should optimize for the "reach" event type. However, if you're mostly interested in driving site visitors or full video views based upon who you reached, through your video campaigns — it makes sense to optimize for the "lowest cpc" event type.

As a part of promotional efforts for NextAfter’s 2022 NIO Summit event, we wanted to experiment with which event we should optimize for as it pertains to video consumption and reaching prospective ticket purchasers.
We set up an experiment on Facebook where one campaign would optimize for “ad recall lift,” while the other campaign was optimized for “reach.”
We were not just looking for reach, though — ultimately we wanted to see how many full video views either campaign would produce.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 100% level of confidence, we increased the reach of our videos to our prospective NIO Summit customers by +12.3%.
Remember, though, we were looking at which campaign event produced the most full video views — and with regards to that metric, we saw inconclusive data, achieving only a +10.2% increase with the "Reach" campaign, but with only an 18% level of confidence.
Both campaigns generated the same number of website clicks, which left us needing to make a decision on which way to go.
Therefore, we decided that rolling out the "Reach" campaign against a new "Lowest CPC" campaign would be our next step in the optimization of this campaign.

experiments.title: How writing a direct subject line increased click rates

rows.name: An Email From a Ukrainian Bomb Shelter
Ukrainian students need your help today

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a direct subject line for email recipients will achieve higher click rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2022 - 04/06/2022
Curiosity is often used in subject lines to improve open rates. Prevailing wisdom states that when an email recipient’s interest is piqued by an unexpected subject line, they almost can’t help but open.
But we wanted to take a closer look at how such an approach would affect click rates—which is, after all, the ultimate goal of email marketing. Specifically, we wanted to know if using curiosity in the subject line might set a false expectation for the reader and thus have a negative impact on click rates. For this experiment, we ran an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test, the treatment that used a more direct subject line resulted in a 5.9% lift in click rate with a 23.1% level of confidence. And while the low level of confidence prevents us from validating the results, we can still draw helpful conclusions from this experiment.
It is interesting to note that  the direct subject line treatment lifted click rates while producing a 6.2% decrease in open rates. This can likely be attributed to two factors:
The subject line, "An email from a Ukrainian Bomb shelter" opens a loop in the recipient's mind, prompting them to open the email to learn more, HOWEVER ...While curiosity improved open rates, it may also have set a false expectation (or failed to set any expectation) in the minds of recipients about the content of the email itself ... whereas the more direct subject line, "Ukrainian students need your help today," while less enticing, sets a much clearer expectation as to what the recipient can expect to find when they open—an appeal to help Ukrainian students.
It is helpful to remember that while open rates are nice (and curiosity subject lines are admittedly fun to write), the ultimate goal of any email that contains a call to action / link is to get the recipient to click through.
When asking email recipients to take a specific action in the body of the email, organizations may be able to improve their click rates by setting a clear expectation in the subject line and thus creating a more consistent experience (and less friction) for potential donors.

This learning can certainly be replicated and applied for future digital appeals, but can also be tested in the acquisition space to see how prospective audiences respond to simplified language compared to the housefile. So we wanted to see if a pretty unique, creative subject line would stand out more.
In this case, we used a treatment that made it seem like we forgot to create the subject line so it was like a note to ourselves to insert a great subject line. We used some square brackets [like this] to call it out further.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With almost a 100% LoC (99.9%) the unique, mystery subject line led to an increase in opens which more than anything underscores how changing up the type of subject lines used can help keep people engaged. In this case, the [square brackets] may have helped it stand out but our theory is the more humourous and unique element of the subject line helped lead to more opens.

Interestingly, with a true 100% LoC (100%) we saw a 93.3% increase in click rate and a 63.8% increase in click to open rate (99.9% LoC) with the unique, mysterious subject line. This underscores both the relationship between the open rate and click rate and how the subject line can influence a reader's motivation before they even get into the body/content of the email itself.

experiments.title: How a relevant subject line affected open rates for a newsletter

rows.name: Russell, I wanted to encourage you today

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding relevancy for a housefile newsletter will achieve increased email engagment.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2021 - 05/03/2021
DTS introduced a new sender for their monthly newsletter and wanted to test how the file would respond to different subject lines. We set up a highly personalized control “Kevin, I wanted to encourage you today” and a highly relevant yet mysterious treatment “Happy Friday!” Neither the control nor the treatment gives away the contents of the email, but both subject lines indicate the message is both authentic from the sender and specifically for you. The treatment adds relevancy by including a day of the week and we wanted to see how this influenced email open rate so we ran a full A/B split test on the monthly newsletter send.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found in our A/B test was that the "Happy Friday" subject line did slightly increase the open rate by 1.3% with 98.7% confidence. However, we also analyzed the click rate of the newsletter, which experienced a 13.9% decrease with a 100% level of confidence. Taking this into consideration, moving forward we will continue to use personalization and mystery in subject lines to maintain and improve overall email engagement. We want the monthly newsletter recipients to not just open the email but to also engage with it and receive value from the contents included. Seeing the small bump in open rate is not nearly as valuable when we simultaneously saw such a decline on the treatment clickthrough rate. Looking ahead, we'd rather have the file be more deeply engaged in a way that they are taking that next step to demonstrate they are interested, connected and invested than to have a 1.3% improvement on the open rate for future newsletters. This is why it's important to fully measure our results.

experiments.title: How increased specificity in a subject line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing specificity increase open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2016 - 10/03/2016
The National Breast Cancer Foundation sent an offer to a list of people who had signed up for their Early Detection Plan app. The offer was a free eBook called What Every Woman Needs To Know that included resources related to early detection of breast cancer.
They decided to test subject lines on this email. The control subject line teased the outcome of the eBook: “Stay informed about your breast health”. They hypothesized that being extremely clear about the offer, even in the subject line, might increase open rate since the list was so highly qualified. So they developed a treatment subject line: “A new early detection guide”.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Though both emails had a relatively low open rate, the treatment subject line produced a 15.8% increase over the control. This shows that increased clarity can increased the perceived value of the email and incentivize readers to open.
“Desired effect” language has proven powerful before, but in this case, clarity trumped persuasion.

experiments.title: How a subject line impacted email opens for members in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a subject line utilizing mystery and personal connection for returning members will achieve an increased open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/31/2021
For an annual membership campaign, we wanted to look at ways to optimize the email envelope for The Heritage Foundation appeals. We had a set of historically used subject lines and wanted to test using a different subject line to drive email engagement. We set up the control as “Here’s the next big challenge” and developed a treatment subject line using only the word “Reconnecting”. The sender and body copy remained the same for each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we saw was the treatment produced a 30.1% increase in open rate for the member segment with 100% level of confidence. Our hypothesis was the treatment would lift open rate since the language utilizes the element of mystery for the receiver to make them ask - What does the sender want to reconnect with me about?
Beyond the email open rate, we made further observations that are noteworthy. Clickthrough rate on the treatment saw a 25.1% lift with100.0% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment produced a 93.6% increase in donor conversion with 100.0% level of confidence.
One thing that is important to keep in mind, however, is this email was randomly split so there may be some imbalance in returning member behavior that could have led to these results on donor conversion. Before leaning too heavily or making conclusions on the lift in donor conversion, we would want to do additional analysis on the two segments to verify it was a true and fair split based on time on file and previous giving history.

experiments.title: How changing the sending domain impacted email engagement rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will switching email sender domains increase email engagement rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2020 - 07/19/2020
Americans for Prosperity had been running a number of petition and survey acquisition offers and produced hundreds of thousands of new email addresses, donors, and revenue through this acquisition process. However, with such a large volume of emails coming through these types of offers, over time they noticed their email engagement rates (deliveries, opens, and clicks) going down.
They had two email sender domains available to them, so instead of sending from the one they’ve been primarily using, they decided to test sending an email from the secondary email sender domain to monitor email engagement rates against the primary domain.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a level of confidence of 100% — we observed a 37.2% increase in email open rates when using the secondary sender domain.
Subsequently, we observed a +49.8% increase in email clickthrough rate for the email used within the test, as well.
If email deliverability is an issue for you, perhaps creating a new sender domain may be a technique you can use to increase email deliverability and engagement over time.
When creating a new email sender domain, it's "reputation" must be built up over time. To do this, it requires that the list be segmented into two types: (1) engaged emails, and (2) disengaged emails. For at least 3-months, this new sender domain should only send emails to the "engaged" list. This will help it establish itself as being of "good quality" when evaluated by mail servers.
This will lead to good inbox rates for all emails sent over time.
While this "warm-up" period of time is underway, continue to send your emails from the original sender domain, but only to the "disengaged" portion of the list.
You can send the same email creative, but it's advised that you lead with emailing the "engaged" file first, wait at least an hour, then send the same email to the "disengaged" list from the original email sender domain.
Ideally, recent opens/clicks from the "disengaged" list should move those contacts to the "engaged" segment (and vise versa for people who continue not to open/click if they were originally placed into the "engaged" list).
All of this will allow you to warm up a different email sender domain and could yield increased results in email delivery, email inboxing rates, which will subsequently lead to increases in open and click rates, as well.

experiments.researchQuestion: Which combination of subject line elements will lead to greater opens?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2020 - 03/18/2020
In this experiment, I was using NextAfter’s 5-step subject line creation tool to craft a subject line for a new webinar. I ended up with 2 different, equally viable subject lines. The first emphasized “utility” or “value” while not giving away the exact topic of the webinar. The second was equally “mysterious” while laying heavily into personalization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The heavily personalized version led to a 25.5% increase in opens at a 99.9% level of confidence. But interestingly enough, there was no statistically significant change in the number of people who signed up for the webinar.
This is yet another example of how the subject line plays a crucial role in setting up how someone will receive and perceive the rest of the email. In this case, it's likely that people opened the treatment because of the additional personalization and mystery, but ultimately weren't inclined to join a webinar. While the opens generated by the control were already primed to engage with a webinar - leading to a higher conversion rate from those that did open the email.
In a case like this where the net signups are a wash - I will always err on the side of the email with greater opens. At the most basic level, getting more opens can lead to better deliverability, and lead to stronger relationships with the folks on our email file.

experiments.title: How using a different sender increased open rates
experiments.researchQuestion: How does our audience respond to our various senders?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/14/2020
Americans for Prosperity has been using two senders to communicate to their house file for slightly more than a year now. Tim, the President of the organization, is the primary solicitor and has historically sent 1-2 weekly emails to promote news and updates to the reader. In the summer of 2019, we added an additional sender who largely cultivates the audience (doesn’t ask) other than in the context of a high urgency campaign (as a follow-up to Tim’s earlier solicitation emails if they haven’t yet responded before the deadline).
We wanted to specifically quantify how the audience responded to the two senders by running an A/B split test on a weekly cultivation email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment validated that the non-soliciting sender achieved a +15% increase in open rate with 99.9% level of confidence.
Beyond the fact that “Matt” (the non-soliciting sender in the program) makes far less fundraising appeals via email than Tim (the primary solicitor in the program), we observed one other note: Matt’s email domain was also different than Tim’s.
Further experimentation is required to specifically identify whether or not the sender domain reputation or the less-likely-to-solicit sender’s name (Matt) in the treatment was what increased open rates. Our theory, though, is that Tim’s email sender domain reputation is lower than the email domain reputation from the email Matt used. For this reason, we expect a higher number of emails to go into the SPAM folder from Tim’s email, which could be a contributing factor to his decrease in open rate.
Finally, it’s also worth noting that even though Matt received a 15% increase in open rate, this sender only achieved a +7.7% increase in article clickthrough rate, but it did not validate with a 44% level of confidence.
What could this mean? It could mean that the audience has been conditioned to expect Tim to send them a link and ask them to do something—and those that open the email are twice as likely to take Tim’s call to action when compared to the less-likely-to-solicit sender (Matt).
Again, further experimentation is required to specifically isolate the sender and the email domain to better understand the learning from this experiment and how best to use this information to improve results moving forward.

experiments.title: How page formatting impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the readability of the articles increase overall visitor engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/15/2019 - 10/01/2019
Alliance Defending Freedom had turned their blog posts into a significant source of new subscribers for the organization. As a result, much of the optimization efforts had been put into increasing that acquisition or the downstream conversion. However, during a discussion with the whole team, a theory was developed that if we could increase engagement on the page, we could potentially increase email acquisition too.
We decided to shift our efforts from direct email acquisition to indirect acquisition through increased engagement. One of the first efforts we decided to test was making the blog more readable. To do this, we increased the default font size and added quotes throughout the articles. Where relevant, we also added sub-headlines to make the text more scannable to the average reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While not a huge percentage, by simply increasing the font-size and adding elements to the page to make it easier to scan, we were able to increase the number of people to read the articles by 1.9%. (This was determined by measuring how many people made it 75% down the page.) Despite the small percentage, this means that roughly 2.5K more people engaged with the content than otherwise would have.
We also saw several other metrics move with this change:
There was a directional 7% increase in email acquisition (at an 80* level of confidence)
3% increase in read rate when we excluded the most popular article that made up 72% of traffic

experiments.title: Does an email sent from an individual produce a higher open rate than an email sent from an organization?
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase open rates of an email by sending from a person rather than an organization?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/14/2018
Traditionally during the Global Gift Guide campaign, emails are sent from World Concern. In an effort to increase the volume of people who open the emails being sent, we hypothesized that an email sent from a person rather than the organization might result in higher open rates. To test this, we developed a treatment email that was sent from Jacinta Tegman. The sender was the only variable that was different for the email; all other email content remained consistent with the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By sending emails from a person rather than an organization, we were able to increase open rates by 38%. People are more motivated to engage with people, not organizations. When a person sees that an email is from an individual, it automatically feels authentic and personal. Emails from organizations usually signify it is a marketing email, or that the organization is wanting something. This leads to lower levels of engagement.
Getting more people to open the email is just the first step in the conversion process. The more people we are able to get to open the email, the more we are able to get to read the content and potentially convert and give. This personal approach in email is a method to continue to test all the way through the email content to see the full impact that it can have on donor conversion.

Ended On: 02/07/2019
Hillsdale College recently sent out an email that offered the opportunity for the recipient to receive a pocket-sized Constitution booklet for absolutely no cost. The initial email was opened and clicked through by many, but a follow-up email was necessary to ensure as many people as possible had the chance to get their free pocket Constitution. Two distinct subject lines were crafted for this follow-up email, and the results regarding the effectiveness of these subject lines were recorded.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As predicted, the treatment subject line, was effective in achieving a higher open rate than the control subject line. In fact, the open rate for the treatment was 2.03% higher than the control. The treatment contained personalization, a synonym for the word “free,” and a call to action instead of a question. The combination of all of these resulted in a higher likelihood of the receiver opening the email.
The main purpose of the experiment was to measure the difference in open rates between the two subject lines, and those results were conclusive. However, the control subject line did achieve a higher click-through rate than the treatment. Looking into why this was the case could be the basis for future research.

experiments.title: How subject line urgency impacted email performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will have the biggest impact on subscriber engagement (opens and clicks)?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2016 - 01/13/2017
As the year end deadline approached for Illinois Policy Institute’s year end fundraising campaign, they wanted to find ways to communicate that urgency to the subscribers. For the second-to-last email send, sent out on December 30th, they decided to run a subject line test to determine which would have the biggest impact on subscriber engagement.
Both of the subject lines communicated urgency. The primary difference between the two was that one, time is running out, is more direct in its approach and is a more common approach taken by many organizations in their fundraising and marketing-style emails. The alternative, tick tock, was a bit less common and did not give away the purpose of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the more direct and “sale-y” subject line of time is running out decreased the open rate by 8%. Additionally, this lower open rate led to an incrementally lower click through rate with a 33% decrease (with 94.6% statistical validity). Our hypothesis is that with the time is running out subject line, the subscribers immediately felt marketed to since it is not a subject line most people would use in their day to day communications. As a result, they were less likely to engage.
This is a great example of the concept that people give to people. As fundraisers, we want to communicate urgency but we need to do it in such a way that it still feels genuine.

experiments.title: How urgency in the subject line impacts open and click rates

For the second email in the series, we wanted to discover if adding urgency to the subject line would impact the percentage of people that either opened or clicked on the message. We decided to test a more vague subject line against one that conveyed the urgency of the December 31st deadline since that was the topic of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In the end, the more urgent subject line of “Don’t wait another minute” decreased the open rate of the email by 27.5%. However, we also wanted to monitor how it impacted the click through rate since the ultimate goal of this email was to get people to the donation page. When analyzed, we discovered there was not a significant difference between the percentage of people clicking on a link.
As a rule of thumb, it is a good trade off to get less opens if it means more people clicking on the link but with there being no discernible difference between click rates, we would rather have more people reading the email so they are exposed to the message when emails later in the series are sent.

We also tested a radical redesign of the email and a completely new copy approach, which also produced a lift in clickthrough rate. This is a reminder of a key principle from the Fundraiser’s Creed: people give to people, not organizations or marketing machines.

experiments.title: Gmail’s handling of full-format vs. low-format emails
experiments.researchQuestion: Does Gmail treat low-format emails differently than full-format emails?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2017 - 08/10/2017
We had already been testing a low-format version of our emails (no tables, no images, limited CSS, visible links) and seeing that they performed several percentage points better in open rate than the fully formatted versions. We suspected that one or more email clients was treating low-format emails differently than full-format emails, and our first suspect was Google due to its tabbed inbox system.
experiments.keyLearnings:
It turns out that, with no changes to email address, subject line or preheader text, Gmail directs low-format emails into the Primary inbox and full-format emails into the Promotions tab. With the email in the Primary inbox, open rates increase by 50-60%. This has held true for nearly a dozen sends over two months.
This indicates that a vast majority of Gmail users don’t know about the Promotions tab or forget to look there. We’ve also determined that most Gmail mobile apps make the Promotions area hard to find, although Google now includes Promotions notifications in the Primary inbox.
As a further note, no other email client shows a statistically significant improvement from a low-format version, and AT&T properties show a negative impact.

Timeframe: 10/16/2017 - 10/17/2017
Live Action was running a high-urgency campaign around Twitter banning their pro-life message and ads from it’s platform. The first email in the campaign was a petition to their housefile asking people to add their name to the list of pro-life advocates. They did a resend of that same email and wanted to test the subject line of the resend to see if they could get more people to open the email, read the contents in it, and click-through to the landing page. The control used a similar approach that we had worked well in a previous campaign, “Did you see Lila’s email?” The treatment used a personalization tactic in the subject line, “[FIRSTNAME], your name is missing from our Twitter petition”.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment increased opens by 4.2%. While the control subject line was engaging, the treatment directly told called the end reader out and told them the purpose of the email. Even though this subject line experiment only slightly impacted open rate, it also impact click-rate. The treatment also helped increase the amount of people clicking through to the landing page because it reinforced the value proposition of the email content.

experiments.title: How the tone of a subject line impacts open rate

rows.name: NEW VIDEO: Prenatal care nonexistent at Planned Parenthood

experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line would get a higher open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/24/2017 - 02/01/2017
Live Action produces investigative videos where they go undercover inside abortion facilities. These investigative videos receive millions of views. Whenever they release a new investigative video, they send it out to their email house file first. Traditionally, the subject line hints at the release of the latest video by saying “NEW VIDEO: Prenatal care nonexistent at Planned Parenthood”. They wandered if the would be able to increase the number of people who opened the email if they took a more casual approach and didn’t mention that the email was a “new video” release. They developed a treatment with the subject line: “One option: abortion:”. They A/B tested the two different subject lines.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment email that didn’t mention the new video in the subject line had a 14% increase in opens. While it might seem strategic to mention the new video in the subject line since that is what the email is about, the tone of the control subject line may have come off too organization-centric causing less people to want to open it. When subject lines are more casual and believable that Lila has actually sent the email, people are more motivated to open them.

experiments.title: How making a subject line personal to the reader impacts open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line would motivate more people to open the email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2017 - 03/13/2017
Some of the content Live Action sends in their emails is story-based and about a particular person who they name and identify in the copy. For Live Action’s recent fundraising email, it was about a person named Emma. Normally they use the name of the person that the email is about in their subject line to give it a more personal approach. For this email, they wanted to test the motivation of the reader and see which subject line would get more opens and ultimately more clicks to their landing page. The two subject lines they tested were “Emma chose life” and “You saved a life”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment subject line, “You saved a life” had a 23.6% increase in opens. Not only did this subject line significantly increase the open rate, it also got a 33% increase in clicks. What we can learn from this experiment is:
1. By saying “You” saved a life, it made the subject line personal to the reader. It also empowered them and caused intrigue into “how” they saved a life resulting in more people wanting to open the email and read it.
2. The treatment subject line reinforced the call-to-action for people to help save more lives. This resulted in more people clicking through to the donation page to give.

experiments.title: How email design affects open rate in a newsletter email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a designed email newsletter affect open rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2019 - 03/18/2019
Email appeal experiments had given us a predisposition to think that email with button, images, and minor design elements are ineffective. But we had really only discovered that to be true in the specific context of a donation appeal.
In this experiment, we wondered if basic design elements could actually lead to greater click-throughs. The control was a plain-text style email in the format we had tested into, and the treatment added section images and clickable html buttons.
But we also wanted to keep an eye on open-rates. We thought it was possible for these design elements to hurt deliverability and inbox placement, and hurt open rates as a result.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This result is a little bit baffling. We expected we might see a decrease in opens, or in the best case scenario, see no change at all. Instead, we saw an 8.1% increase in open rates at a 98% level of confidence.
We thought it was possible that the preview text appeared differently, but that proved to not be the case. Subject lines and senders were also exactly the same. Now, the 98% confidence means theres a 1 in 50 chance that this result may not hold in when tested again. The odds of that are very slim, but seeing that we have no real explanation for why this result occurred, we will test again to be sure.

experiments.title: How assumption of desire for an offer affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will assuming the recipient wants an offer affect open rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2019
Hillsdale College was sending complimentary pocket Constitutions to their constituents and donors. They knew they had a list of people who had not received a free pocket Constitution, but they weren’t sure if the people wanted a pocket Constitution. They decided to test two different approaches in the subject line. The first approach asked a question—”Do you want a free pocket Constitution?” The second assumed that they did want it, and offered them to them: “Get your complimentary pocket Constitution”. They split the file and sent a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The subject line that assumed the recipient wanted the offer produced a 30% lift in open rate. There are two possible reasons for this. First, the question format allowed the recipient to decide whether or not to open the email before they even opened the email. If they didn't want it, many people may have just archived or deleted the email. Second, the two subject lines also used two different words to describe the offer: "free" and "complimentary". This may have had an impact, as "free" implies that the offer is widely available and of limited value, while "complimentary" implies that the offer is of some value but is given free of charge due to a relationship. This element should be specifically targeted to determine impact in a future test.

Buckner International was sending out their monthly cultivation email to their house file. In recent months, one of the primary discussion points related to their subscribers revolved around how we could increase their overall engagement. The open rate of the file had slipped in recent months.
In addition to evaluating technical solutions to the problem, we also wanted to see if we could identify topics that may reactivate people. We will test this further in March by running a multi-email experiment using a segment that will receive solicitations with scriptural references and comparing their giving to a segment that does not have any scripture in their communications.
