experiments.title: How simplifying an email template improved open rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simple plain text style email improve open and/or clickthrough rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/09/2018
In reviewing historical promotional emails for the Global Gift Guide, it was clear that the organization has always leaned upon well designed and graphic-heavy images to promote the gift catalog. Our research on the subject has typically yielded improved results when stripping email templates down to “plain text” style emails (still with complete tracking and HTML-based) to appear as if they were written from an individual.
We wanted to experiment with emails in 2018 to verify if this approach would also work for World Concern.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 98% confidence score, this experiment validated that "plain text" style emails improved open rates by 35% for World Concern.
Technologically speaking, highly stylized and image-heavy emails are typically "caught" by the email server that receives them before sending them to the intended recipient, and have a higher chance of being codified as a spam message. This plain text style template not only has shown to improve engagement rates because people give to people, not organizations, it also typically yields much higher open and click volumes due to the higher inboxing rates.
When possible, always find a way to send message from a person at your organization to your donors.

We typically let the preview text pull in the opening lines of the email since our emails are always personally written. We wondered if specifically crafting preview text to emphasize the value of the content in the email could increase opens and clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that this specifically crafted preview text actually decreased opens by 8.8%. Our hypothesis here is that preview text that sounds and looks like marketing is a dead giveaway that this is a marketing email. We've removed an aspect of the personal touch, and this is sets off a flag in the recipients mind that this email is inauthentic.

When looking at clicks, we saw no significant change. So even though we saw a slightly higher click-to-open rate from the more value heavy preview text, the net effect on clicks was a wash.

More importantly, when a subject line becomes personal or sounds like the sender is personally emailing the recipient, people are even more motivated to open it. The email starts to feel less like a marketing appeal and more like an email you would receive from someone who knows you. It is important to talk to donors and prospective donors like they are people – even as early on in the communication process as the subject line.

experiments.title: How inviting an email recipient to participate affects email open rate

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2017 - 12/13/2017
Hillsdale College recently began their calendar year end campaign. This year they decided to focus more specifically on their independence and how it enables them to fulfill their mission–without accepting EVEN ONE PENNY of government funding! One of their appeal emails focused on their presence in Washington D.C. through the Kirby Center. Before sending, they wondered: Will recipients be more motivated to open an email that invites them to participate in an action (in this case, helping teach policymakers about the constitution). They created an A/B subject line test and monitored the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale College found that the treatment version actually decreased email open rate by about 5%. Though the relative impact of this test was small, it provides a valuable learning for future email subject line creation. Though the invitation to ‘Help teach policymakers the Constitution’ feels like a noble thing to ask recipients, it’s clear that it halted some in their inbox. It’s likely that the use of the word  ‘help’ struck some as a plea. This word makes people feel like the impetus is on them to do something. This can produce unnecessary anxiety in the mind of the recipient before they even have a chance to read the email.
This is an important thing to remember for any organization that is running a calendar year end campaign, and it will continue to inform Hillsdale College’s approach to email communication.

In their fourth email appeal, they highlighted Imprimis–their popular monthly speech digest that has a wider readership than the Wall Street Journal. Before sending this appeal, they wondered: Will increasing the relevancy of the subject line increase email open rate? They created a treatment version with the words ‘Fake News’ and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Hillsdale College found that the treatment version did increase email open rate by nearly 9%. It’s clear that the use of the term ‘Fake News’ attracted more attention, and therefore, increased motivation of the email recipients. This is important to remember when crafting subject lines during anytime of the year, but especially during a calendar year end campaign. Email recipients are flooded with emails during the last month of the year, and it’s easy to get lost in the shuffle. You can increase your email open rates by using terms that recipients immediately recognize and have an interest in, as long as they are directly related to the topic you are discussing in the email copy.

Timeframe: 05/04/2016 - 06/15/2016

They sent 10% of their house file each treatment and collected data to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the personalized subject line produced a 22.5% lift in open rate, which showed that including the recipient’s first name in the subject line increases open rate.

The combination of these two factors led to a significant increase in open rate, which helped inform future subject line copy. However, the “trick” of personalization also may come with a significant diminishing return if overused.

experiments.title: How the sender’s title impacted open and clickthrough rates on a weekly newsletter
experiments.researchQuestion: How will including the sender’s org/title in the sender name field impact email open, click and unsubscribe rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On:
As a part of continuous experimentation with increasing engagement from their weekly newsletter, The Fund for American Studies had recently tested into switching senders, but wanted to experiment with whether or not it was important to keep the sender’s title/department as a part of their name in following emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After 4 weekly sends were combined together, we could not validate a lift (or decrease) in any of the metrics we were tracking (open rate, clickthrough rate, or unsubscribe rate).
We did see a slight (albeit not valid) lift in the open rate (and each subsequent metric) when we did not use the sender’s org/title within the “sender name” field, but even so, the difference between the control/treatment was so negligible (at/below 3% across all three metrics), it’s safe to assume that it’s of no great consequence (or threat) to use either variation.
Our recommendation is to always err on the side of being a real person speaking to one individual recipient, and therefore would default to not including the org name/title as a part of the sender name (because most people don’t send emails with that included), however, there is no data in this particular study that would substantiate a significant gain in going that route.

experiments.title: How does increasing the relevancy of a subject line affect email opens
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the relevancy of a subject line for cultivation email recipients will achieve an increase in email engagement because the timeliness of the message is increased.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/12/2023
The Navigators sent a cultivation email as apart of their calendar year end campaign that offered a Christmas Reading Plan for free to their housefile. The original subject line included exclusivity for the user as it said “A Christmas Reading Plan Just for You!” While we’ve seen exclusivity in copy perform well, we wanted to test how changing the subject line to be more timely to the message would affect email open rate. The new subject line read ” A must-read before Christmas!”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Changing the subject line to be more timely and relevant to the message can lead to an increase in email engagement.

2. But while the treatment showed a slight increase in opens, the statistical level of confidence was below the threshold of 85%, indicating that the results are not valid.

To improve future experimentation, testing different variations of subject lines and analyzing the impact on email engagement can provide further insights. It is also crucial to track other engagement metrics such as click-through rate and conversion rate to get a holistic view of the success of email campaigns.

experiments.title: How presentation of a sender name impacted open rates on a survey
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the organization’s full name from the sender line for survey takers will achieve an increase in open rates because the email recipients will believe it is from a real person, not an organization.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2023
In previous testing we had seen a lift in removing the organization’s name from the sender line but we wanted to test this same concept with a larger audience. For the control, we included both title and organization name. For the treatment, we used the organization’s initials and the sender title.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that removing the organization's full name from the sender line for survey takers may result in a slight increase in open rates. However, since the statistical level of confidence is 31%, the results cannot be considered statistically valid. Therefore, it is recommended to conduct further testing with larger sample sizes to validate the results before making any decisions based on them. We did also see a 6.9% decrease in clicks on the treatment with 78.9% level of confidence, so we need to do further testing to see how the sender name could have impacted clickthrough rate conversion.

experiments.title: How stating the “deadline” in a subject line for a high urgency campaign impacted open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving clarity to an email subject line amid a high urgency campaign for email recipients will achieve an increase in email open rate because recipients will know their exact window to take action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2023 - 04/13/2023
The Illinois Policy Institute is amid a high urgency fundraising campaign. The campaign deadline is tomorrow, and we want to test how making that clear in the subject line of today’s email impacts open rate.
The control variant (subject line A) is “Urgent reminder” while the treatment variant (subject line B) is “Deadline tomorrow”. We’ve split these variants to where 50% of our recipients will experience one subject line and 50% will experience the other.
We want to find out how our recipients respond to each subject line in terms of choosing to open the email. We expect “Deadline tomorrow” to produce the most opens because it clearly communicates the deadline to recipients and the fact that the current opportunity will expire after tomorrow.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment variant, which had the subject line "Deadline tomorrow", did not perform better than the control variant, which had the subject line "Urgent reminder". The treatment variant saw a 4.8% decrease in Opens for All Traffic with a 70.5% statistical level of confidence, indicating that these results cannot be considered valid. Therefore, we cannot conclude that giving clarity to the subject line in a high urgency fundraising campaign leads to an increase in email open rate.
It's also worth noting that the treatment subject line produced a 32.3% drop in click rate with an 82.2% level of confidence.
The "Deadline tomorrow" subject line may have been less effective because recipients didn't know what the deadline was for. Instead of opening the email to find out, they may have decided it wasn't worth their time to find out. The "Urgent reminder" SL on the other hand, tells the recipient that they've been told about the opportunity before and they may then feel more inclined to open the email see what they need to be "reminded" about.
From a psychological standpoint, there is also the possibility that the word "deadline" is a turn off to recipients. Humans often view deadlines in a negative context because they're used to hitting deadlines at work or with tasks in life. They may not take kindly to the mention of another deadline.
This angle is worth testing again for a future high urgency email to get more data behind it.

experiments.title: How emailing for a well-known sender in a high urgency campaign impacted email engagement from the housefile
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emails from a well-known sender during a high urgency campaign for housefile subscribers will achieve an increase in opens and clicks.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
During a high urgency campaign, we wanted to test multiple senders to assess how the housefile responded to emails from different team members. Because they receive a weekly cultivation email from the Vice President of Development, we thought we might see higher engagement on emails sent from him during the campaign rather than a member of the Membership Programs team since they are familiar with hearing from him regularly.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not have a large enough sample size to validate the test. We saw a slight decrease in the open rate on the treatment email but only with 46% level of confidence. Going forward, we will want to also measure the click rate and donation conversion rate for each sender to see if there is any significant difference in response.

experiments.title: How a problem-specific subject line impacted email engagement in a high urgency campaign

rows.name: Treatment - The Left is relentless. So are you.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding mystery to the subject line for the housefile in a high urgency campaign will achieve an increase in the open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
During a high urgency campaign, most of the subject lines were in reference to membership and were relevant to the campaign. We wanted to find out if a problem-oriented subject line that held some mystery about the contents of the message would increase open and click rates for the email. We split the file 50/50 and send the control subject line “About your membership” vs. “The Left is relentless. So are you.” both from the same sender.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not validate the experiment since we only reached a 16% level of confidence that there was a .24% increase in the open rate on the treatment. Since there was no significant change observed, we will have to test this concept again on future emails or try other methods to improve the email engagement rate during a high urgency campaign with frequent emails. We want to find out if the housefile is more responsive to membership-specific messages concerning their status or if they are more intrigued by the problem we are facing and need their help to solve.

experiments.title: How specificity in a subject line impacts open rate

rows.name: You should read this with your family
You should read this to your kids

experiments.researchQuestion: Will specificity in the subject line increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/11/2020 - 11/24/2020
Care Net was sending a cultivation email to their file and wanted to test the element of specificity in their subject line. The control was “You should read this with your family” and the treatment was, “You should read this with your kids”.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren't able to validate the results, but we did see a directional lift from the treatment. This tells us that the specificity from the treatment minimally helped increase engagement, but wasn't a significant enough difference to have a greater impact.

experiments.title: How changing the email sender affects a time-sensitive membership renewal campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the email sender for the housefile email list will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
For an annual membership campaign, we wanted to look at ways to optimize the email envelope for The Heritage Foundation appeals. We tested the open rate for emails from two different senders. The control email included Maggy Smith – a representative from Membership Programs – as the sender, and the treatment email included Kay C. James – President of The Heritage Foundation – as the sender. The subject line and body copy was the same for each email.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although there was a 1.6% increase in traffic, the experiment failed to validate at a 79.9% level of confidence. Users may be more inclined to open an email from the president of the company than a representative of Membership Programs; however, the data does not show a significant impact. This idea would need to be re-tested in the future.

experiments.title: How a relevant subject line affected housefile engagement for a cultivation email

rows.name: Are you familiar with Mattress Mack?

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing “you” to add relevance in a subject line for a cultivation series to the housefile will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
We were noticing a recent decline in open rate for a weekly cultivation email series to the housefile so we wanted to test some subject line elements to see how we could improve how the audience was responding to different subject lines and increase engagement with the cultivation emails. We established a control—“An interesting interview”—using the element of mystery to invoke curiosity and a treatment—“Are you familiar with Mattress Mack?”—using the word you to create relevancy for the email recipients. We set up the A/B split test to see which of the two elements had better performance so we can continue to adjust subject lines for future emails in the series by using what works for this particular audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was set up in Hubspot to monitor results for the first 4 hours after the email test was sent to 50% of the total audience and then send the email with the most opens to the remainder of the file. Because of this, we did not have a large enough sample size to validate the experiment. The treatment saw a 6.3% decrease in traffic, but we did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence to draw any conclusions based on these results. Moving forward, we will want to run a similar test as a split to the full housefile rather than just half of it so we can validate results and find out how to best optimize the subject lines for this audience.

experiments.title: How changing the sender name in a membership email affects the open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a different sender name for the housefile audience will achieve a higher open rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test different sender names to measure open and click response. We wanted to test the effect a different sender name would have on the open rate of an email. The control email was from “Maggy” while the treatment was from “Andrew.” The audience is accustomed to receiving membership emails from “Maggy” and weekly cultivation emails from “Andrew.”
experiments.keyLearnings: Though there was a .74% decrease in traffic this experiment does not validate because only a 47.3% confidence level was achieved. Further testing and a larger sample size would be needed in order to validate these results. We recommend sending from another person in the organization to see if that affects the open rate since the housefile is accustomed to seeing emails from both "Maggy" and "Andrew."

experiments.title: How changing the sender name affects the open rate of an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the sender name for the housefile audience will achieve a greater open rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test sender names to measure open and click response. We tested two different names from the organization in order to see how each name would affect the open rate of an email. We used “Maggy Smith” for the control email and “Andrew McIndoe” for the treatment. “Andrew” sends a weekly cultivation email while “Maggy” is the usual sender for membership card appeals.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though we saw a 1% increase in traffic, this test did not validate because it resulted in a confidence level of 66.8%. However, a retest with a larger sample size is recommended in order to validate results. There was also no statistical difference in the click thru rates for each email. We recommend potentially using another person's name to see if it would significantly increase open rates since the housefile is used to seeing both "Andrew" and "Maggy" in their inbox.

experiments.title: How a subject line test impacted open rates on a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a new subject line for returning donors will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test various subject lines to measure open and click response. We developed a treatment that used more active rather than passive language. The control subject line asked the question “Are you ready?” while the treatment asked “Are you coming?” while the body copy remained the same for both versions.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see any valid results from the experiment and only reached 50.2% level of confidence so this concept would need to be re-tested in the future. Looking at the body copy for the treatment, we would want to also test aligning the body copy with the subject line to make sure the original question is addressed or expounded upon to help the message flow more congruently.

experiments.title: How a more humanized subject line affects open rate on an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a humanized subject line for a housefile audience will achieve an increase in open rates .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test various subject lines to measure open and click response. We wanted to test the effect of a more humanized subject line vs. a standard subject line in an email. The control subject line read “Membership question” and the treatment read “I hope you will be joining us.”
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a .69% increase in traffic on the treatment but this test did not validate because it resulted in a confidence level of 49.2%. This would require retesting and tested with a prospecting email instead of a housefile email in order to test a larger sample size. We also recommend testing a more humanized subject line again using a first name in the subject line instead of "you" if testing the housefile email again.

experiments.title: How changing the sender name affected the open rate on an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the sender for housefile email recipients will achieve increased open rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement, we wanted to test different email senders to measure open rate. We wondered if users would be more inclined to open an email from one sender than another. The control sender was “Maggy Smith” and the treatment sender was “Andrew P. McIndoe.” Maggy works in Membership Programs and Andrew is the Vice President of Development. Each email contains the same subject line and body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment resulted in a -0.59 decrease in traffic and a 39.0% level of confidence. Users engaged less with Andrew P. McIndoe than they did with Maggy Smith. A possible reason is users are more familiar with Maggy Smith, as they've heard from her before about membership relations. Also, an email from a representative of Membership Programs may be more engaging than an email from the Vice President of Development, assuming the user is familiar with each.

The next experiment could be to change the subject line to something more specific, such as "Renew your membership today" rather than "I haven't heard from you." The user may be more inclined to open the email because of a needed action, regardless of the sender.

experiments.title: How a social proof slider showing who just signed a petition impacted petition signature rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does showing who just signed a petition in a notification window to petition page viewers increase petition signature rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/29/2020 - 09/20/2020
As a part of Canadian Taxpayer’s efforts to increase petition signature rates, we decided to experiment with using social proof to create momentum for those considering signing the petition. We decided to show petition page viewers (who had not yet signed the petition) a treatment that displayed a notification in the bottom/left side of their screen showing the first name & province location of the person that just recently signed the petition.
If others also signed the petition while they stayed on the page, new slide-out notifications with the next signer’s information would display to the person on the page, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for nearly a month and collecting 12,000+ total signatures, we could discern no noticeable difference between the two treatments.

What does this mean? It means that the notification window doesn't do enough to create momentum to improve petition signature rates.

Our overall team did acknowledge that perhaps showing personal details was a "turn off" for some, as much as it might have pushed more to sign, as privacy is really important to some folks in the Canadian Taxpayer's audience.

This theory is testable, so further experimentation with address data security and privacy is a follow-up experiment we are looking to run next.

experiments.title: How including a hot button issue in the subject line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will mentioning free speech in the subject line improve the open rate of the email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/13/2018 - 07/14/2018
The Leadership Institute works with college students to help defend their free speech on campus. They wanted to see if including hot button terms like “free speech” and “the one percent” in the subject line would affect open rates. Their hypothesis was that the subject line about free speech would generate more opens.
experiments.keyLearnings: The open rates for each email were no more than .4% deviations away from 20%. This experiment was not conclusive. It’s likely that the hot button issues did not affect open rate as much due to the specified name of the school in the subject line. A further experiment without a specified name may render desired results to confidently conclude this experiment.

experiments.title: How mystery impacted open rates of a webinar invitation email to high profile donors
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding an element of mystery to the subject line increase open rates from these high profile donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/13/2020
Americans for Prosperity was looking to experiment with what ways they could increase high profile donor email engagement when promoting an upcoming webinar for them to attend. Because there was no way to register in the body of the email (but rather just “save the day/time”), the primary metric to measure was the open rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Regardless of which treatment these high profile donors were sent, the results were essentially identical, achieving only a 0.64% difference, with a 3.65% level of confidence (LOC).
At least when it comes to high profile VIP donors, that it doesn’t matter if you use mystery in your subject line to increase open rates.

experiments.title: Will an emoji in the subject line help increase open rate?

rows.name: Fundraising Content You’ll Love… see what I did there…
Fundraising Content You’ll ❤️… see what I did there…

experiments.researchQuestion: Will an emoji in the subject line help increase open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2020 - 02/28/2020
People use emoji’s all the time and we get asked how they can or should be used in emails and subject lines. We didn’t have any good experiments available to justify our thinking that it isn’t a worthwhile test so decided to test it for ourselves. We were sending a digest email on Valentines day so tied in the subject line to ‘love’ for the control and for the treatment we used a heart emoji in its place.
experiments.keyLearnings: Short answer: nope, didn’t do anything. With a 25% level of confidence, the emoji didn’t seem to have any impact on the likelihood of a subscriber to open.

experiments.title: How changing the sender name from Fr. David P. Uribe, OMI to Fr. David increases open rates for Daily Prayer
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the name from Fr. David P. Uribe, OMI to Fr. David increase open rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2019
We’ve previously tested the sender name for other emails and found that using Fr. David increased open rates, but we wanted to see if it would do the same for our Daily Prayer that we send out every morning.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that using Fr. David did increase open rates, but not as much as our regular email did. We think because these recipients are so used to seeing the subject line Daily Prayer every morning that the sender name might not be as important to them.

experiments.title: How changing Fr. David P. Uribe, OMI to just Fr. David increases open rates for Daily Prayer
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the name from Fr. David P. Uribe, OMI to Fr. David increase open rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2019
We’ve previously tested the sender name for other emails and found that using Fr. David increased open rates, but we wanted to see if it would do the same for our Daily Prayer that we send out every morning.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that using Fr. David did increase open rates, but not as much as our regular email did. We think because these recipients are so used to seeing the subject line Daily Prayer every morning that the sender name might not be as important to them.

experiments.title: How indicating the time commitment of an email impacts open rate

rows.name: Why the "village" can't replace parents (4 min read)

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the time commitment to the subject line increase open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2018 - 11/10/2018
For the weekly cultivation email sent out by Care Net, we wanted to see if we could increase the email engagement by letting subscribers know the time commitment required to consume the topic. This is something often seen on blog posts but we wanted to see if it would have a positive impact in email. To do this, we added ” (4 min read)” to the end of the subject line.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a nearly identical open rate between the two versions. After further reflection, it is our hypothesis that the longer subject line may have resulted in the “(4 min read)” not being shown in the inbox. Having this aspect cut off would have resulted in the two variants being identical.

experiments.title: How switching the email subject line to place the words gift and prayer first increases opens
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate

rows.name: Enroll a Loved One Today - Share the Gift of Prayer
Share the Gift of Prayer - Enroll a Loved One Today

experiments.researchQuestion: The question that we we’re wanting to answer is if this worked for every email. Would people be more willing to click if they noticed gift and prayer first in the subject line or would they still click on the email if that was towards the end of the subject line.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2019
We tested this through Mailchimp. We’ve noticed in previous tests and throughout our website analytics that most of the donors respond more when they see the words gift or prayer.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key takeaway is that the results were almost completely identical. This could be because we also had words like Loved Ones at the beginning of the email that also usually generates a lot of interest with our audience. Moving forward when running a test like this we will have to make sure there is no words that could make an impact emotionally like Loved Ones is.

experiments.title: How a subject line test of a famous author’s first novel vs the root of their creative genius affected opens

rows.name: The roots of Jane Austen's genius

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a subject line test of a famous author’s first novel vs the root of their creative genius increase opens?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2018 - 04/20/2018
This experiment was conducted to see if a change in subject line between Jane Austen’s first novel and the root of her creative genius had any effect on open rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: The open rate for the control was 14% and the open rate for the treatment was 14.1%. This means there was no significant difference in responses to the subject lines. For future experiments, we will need to test more radical changes to the subject line.

experiments.title: Does sending a newsletter on Friday instead of Saturday morning improve open rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does sending a newsletter on Friday instead of Saturday morning improve open rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/12/2018
AFP wanted to know if sending their weekly newsletter on Friday morning improved open rates over their standard Saturday morning delivery timeframe.
experiments.keyLearnings: Inconclusive. We need to create a larger sample and run the split again to conclusively declare a winner.

experiments.title: How the an individual sender impacted open rate for a cold list
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an individual sender increase the open rate for a prospecting email list?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/16/2018
Alliance Defending Freedom was in the process of cultivating a new list of email prospects with a series of emails. We had noticed a declining open rate so we wanted to see if there was a way to potentially boost this metric. We decided to try sending the emails from an individual (Michael Farris) instead of the organization. We had the hypothesis that people would be more likely to respond to an individual than an organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this situation, we did not see a statistically significant difference between either sender. This is an interesting observation because with previous experiments for ADF related to sender name, we found that individuals will often perform better for their existing housefile. This would suggest that it isn’t just sending from an individual that moves the needle but sending from a known individual.

experiments.title: How email design impacted opens with a prospecting email list
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simpler email increase the likelihood of the email being opened?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2018 - 05/25/2018
ADF was using their existing email welcome series to “warm up” the purchased prospecting list from Axciom. The open rate of this list was significantly low compared to the standard house file emails so we decided to test some deliverability tactics to improve this metric. We had found the sender did not play a significant roll in increasing the opens so we decided to test the style of the email. The hypothesis was that a simpler email would not be placed in the “Promotions” tab of systems like Gmail and Yahoo which would increase the likelihood of being opened.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant different in open rate between both the designed and plain text emails. This is actually beneficial for ADF as their standard emails to their subscriber base have the more “designed” style which means this email file will fit well within their audience.

experiments.title: How including the brand name impacts email opens
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including the organization’s name in the sender information increase email opens?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/19/2018
As part of a stewardship series for recent donors, Buckner was sending an email that included some stories of people that were impacted by the ministries. This email was going to donors from the last 12 months. We decided to run a quick test to see if including the organization’s name in the sender information would increase brand familiarity and, thus, boost open rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw no statistically significant difference in the sender that incorporated “Buckner International” into the sender. This tells us the brand doesn’t necessarily boost engagement but it also doesn’t get in the way.
We always strive to humanize the marketing emails that get sent out but, at the same time, we have to try different techniques to increase subscriber engagement. That is why experiments like this are important with “minor” sends like this. We can use these messages to determine what works best for the audience and can then apply it to more critical emails like those that are a part of fundraising campaigns.
